CryptoQuant analyst Maartunn recently provided a perspective on Bitcoin market dynamics through an analysis of user activity on Binance, the world’s largest cryptocurrency exchange by trading volume.

Maartunn’s investigation into Binance user retention patterns offered some interesting insights into trading behavior that could influence Bitcoin’s market performance.

According to the analyst’s findings, over half of returning Binance users make their second deposit within 16 days following their initial transaction. Nearly 10% of users perform their second deposit within just one day, indicating active trading behavior rather than passive investment strategies.

Furthermore, approximately one-third of returning users reload their accounts by day seven, reinforcing the notion that Binance predominantly attracts traders engaging in frequent transactions.

CryptoQuant.com
@cryptoquant_com

Over 50% of Returning Binance Users Make a Second Deposit Within 16 Days

“Some interesting takeaways:

Nearly 10% return on Day 1, showing immediate trading interest. By Day 7, one-third of users have already reloaded. By Day 16, over 50% of returning users have made a… pic.twitter.com/OG6d6BKUdt

This high frequency of early deposits by returning users highlights a pattern of short-term trading rather than long-term holding. Such active engagement on Binance can directly impact Bitcoin’s price volatility, as rapid buying and selling contribute significantly to market fluctuations.

Increased trading activity shortly after initial deposits often implies speculative market behavior, potentially leading to quick price movements in both directions.

Overall, the behavioral trends observed on Binance suggest that Bitcoin might continue to experience sharp volatility in the near term. The quick return rate of traders to deposit funds signals a market where trading volume spikes are frequent, influencing Bitcoin’s price stability.

data.title: These Schools Are Missing Out on the March Madness Final Four, But Their Endowments Are Winners
data.description:
By Andy Serwer

If the Big Ten schools didn't fare so well in March Madness this year, there's always Jubilant June to look forward to.

Using the most recent data available, PitchBook, the data company owned by Morningstar, has just introduced a dashboard tracker showing the returns of the fifty largest college and university endowments, many of which have fiscal years ending in June.

The top three schools last fiscal year according to the tracker? How about Michigan State (Spartans!), 15.1%; Wisconsin (Badgers!), 14.7%; and Minnesota (Gophers!), 13.5%. All of these institutions run smaller funds than the ginormous ones managed by many Ivy League schools.

So take that, Harvard, up 9.6%, and Yale, up 5.7%! (The average return for the largest endowments was 9.4%.)

Yale is particularly relevant here, because it was the home of the late David Swensen who ran that university's endowment. Swenson created what's known as the Yale model, which suggests that university endowments should apportion a sizable stake in non-public investments, such as venture capital, real estate, or private equity.

Perhaps ironically, not subscribing to Swensen's thinking seems to have served the corn-fed, Midwestern-bred schools well. "Some of these smaller endowments have higher allocations to public equities because they haven't fully adopted the Yale model," says PitchBook senior funds analyst Jessica Hamlin. That strategy worked out for some schools, as the S&P 500 gained 23% from the end of June 2023 through June 2024.

Hamlin says that it may be that those smaller, better-performing endowments "aren't really able to get into the big fund names like Blackstone, Apollo and Aries," some of which have had subpar returns, and instead invested in middle-market private deals which had better numbers. Looks like the less elite you were for the 2024 fiscal year, the better.

One-year returns are relatively meaningless, particularly in the long-game world of endowments. And yet, those Big 10 schools held up pretty well over the all-important longer haul as well, with Michigan State and Minnesota beating the 8.3% average return over ten years — though Wisconsin lagged. (None of the three endowments returned calls by press time.)

It's true, of course, that public equities are getting killed this year, which could hurt funds still heavily weighted that way. However, the S&P is down just 1.1% from the end of last June — and who knows what will happen between now and this June.

Among the schools playing in the men's Final Four this weekend — Auburn, Duke, Houston, and Florida — only Duke has an endowment tracked by PitchBook. The Blue Devils' endowment posted an 8% return last fiscal year, certainly a decent number, but a tad below the average.

Duke basketball fans, however, may argue that winning the NCAA championship would offset that.
